speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Explain this document to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This document is quite an irony, because my father was awarded this honorary-- honorary cross in 1934, by the fuhrer, which is Adolf Hitler, and the [GERMAN] on July the 15th, 1935, stating the fact that he participated in the war of 1914, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you read that and translate it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now what does that mean in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: It means in English that Adolf Hitler, in the name of the [GERMAN], has awarded to the dentist, Henry Chaim, this honorary cross in honor of him participating in the World War I, 1914, 1918. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's about it. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of the documents that my father had to submit and get answers to in order to try to get his business practice permission to work again. And one of the reasons given in this particular document here is that you are not-- you are [GERMAN]. You are not a German. And then on the bottom here, it says-- stop it right there-- that [GERMAN]. You did not serve with a fighting unit in the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did he receive this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was done in 1935, at the time where they took my father's business away-- that he could not practice officially. And he tried to get it big-- back, stating that, well. Look! I was a fighter in the first world war. I was in the army and all that. And he got injured. He lost his hearing. But they denied it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else on there that you'd like to read in German and translate?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think that's-- that kind of makes it clear that-- the first paragraph, where it says, [GERMAN]. You are not a German-- chapter closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is it. This is the certificate that we were able to acquire for the torrendous-- horrendous sum of $5 US. The Japanese Consulate-General of Shanghai gave us permission to enter the-- this is to certify that permission to enter into the Japanese-- Japanese-occupied part of the international settlement in Shanghai, north of Soochow Creek. That was Hongkew. This particular one was issued to Chaim, Erwin, Israel. In Germany, you had to have the middle initial-- Israel for the men and Sarah for the woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now staying in-- in Berlin, and I was the nephew of Mr. Moritz Dessen, who was the one who preceded us in Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you have to have the middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: To make sure that everybody knows we are Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taking on a roof, where the colleague of my father lived-- my father, Henry Chaim, on the right side, and Richard Aronsch, who was also a dentist, in Shanghai. The only place where it was comfortable was up in the open air and on the top floor on the-- on the roof garden, if you want to call it that. They are playing cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This is a picture taken shortly after we arrived in Shanghai, China, in April of 1940. On the far right is my father. Then comes my sister, Evelyn, with her doll in her hand, my mother, Elsie, and then the next two gentleman are the brothers, Adolf and Oskar Dessen, who married two sisters named Hannah and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Lucien. Lucien.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and Lucien. Two brothers married two sisters. Those were the people that got us the certificate to go to Shanghai. And last but not least is myself, in short pants and sandals-- Erwin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken about a month or two after we arrived in Shanghai. This picture depicts the family as we looked about a month before we left Shanghai, again, April of 1947. The far right is my sister, Evelyn. Then comes my father, Heinrick, or Henry Chaim, my mother, Elsie Chaim, and then myself, Erwin, in army uniform. I was not in the army as a-- as an army personnel, but I was in the army as a civilian. That's why I wore those kind of clothes. And then down at the bottom, we had a little dog called Teddy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who's this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a family picture again-- my father on the right, Heinrick Chaim, my sister, Evelyn, my mother, Elsie, and then myself, Erwin-- I would say a 1/2 a year after we arrived in Denver, Colorado. We lived at that time with the Kirsteins, in the basement apartment, at 1641 Irving Street.
